pattern: Carry a running prefix sum down each root-to-node path and count, with a hash map, how many earlier prefixes leave exactly the target behind — turning a quadratic path search into one linear sweep.
hints: A path can start at any node and must go strictly downward, so think about every root-to-current-node prefix and the sums between two points on it.
If you track the running sum from the root to the current node, a downward path ending here sums to target exactly when an earlier prefix equals (current running sum) minus target.

Keep a map of prefix-sum -> how many ancestors produced it (seed it with 0 -> 1); at each node add map[runningSum - target] to the count, record this prefix, recurse, then remove it on the way back up.

edge_inputs.description: several downward paths reach the target
zero-valued nodes and target zero create multiple distinct counted paths
negative values let a longer path return to the target

stress.description: random tree with small values and a small target so many paths qualify, exercising prefix-sum bookkeeping
stress.note: small magnitudes keep many qualifying paths while staying within constraints

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def pathSum(self, root, targetSum):
        from collections import defaultdict
        prefix = defaultdict(int)
        prefix[0] = 1
        self.count = 0
        def dfs(node, running):
            if not node:
                return
            running += node.val
            self.count += prefix[running - targetSum]
            prefix[running] += 1
            dfs(node.left, running)
            dfs(node.right, running)
            prefix[running] -= 1
        dfs(root, 0)
        return self.count
